preFix.title: Managing coccidiosis in lambs
preFix.subtitle: Identifying the signs of coccidiosis, treating affected lambs, and reducing contamination pressure on the rest of the group.
preFix.excerpt: Coccidiosis is a protozoan infection of the intestinal lining that kills lambs at 3 to 8 weeks of age, most often in housed or confined flocks where oocyst build-up is high. Watery, sometimes bloody, scours are the warning sign. Early treatment and good hygiene management prevent the losses from becoming serious.
preFix.sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb factsheet: Coccidiosis in Sheep (2022). DEFRA Code of Welfare for Sheep (2023). APHA disease surveillance reports.

content.text: Coccidiosis
in lambs peaks at three to eight weeks of age, when maternal immunity begins to wane and the lamb is ingesting solid matter. The disease is almost always a problem of housing or penning, where
oocysts
accumulate in warm wet bedding and are swallowed by lambs seeking food or warmth. Outdoor systems with rotating pasture suffer far less than housed systems with static bedding.
Recognising the signs

Watch for: watery dark-brown or bloody scours (not the yellow scour of E. coli or the grey scour of rotavirus); a tucked-up abdomen and dull demeanour; lambs losing condition despite feeding. Affected lambs may continue suckling but pass loose faeces immediately. In a group of 10 or more, the first case will be followed by others within 24-48 hours if not treated.
Treatment
Treat the affected lamb and all other lambs in the same pen with
diclazuril
at the dose rate on the bottle. Use a
drenching gun

and give the dose over the back of the tongue. A single treatment stops new oocyst development; do not retreat within 28 days unless a new outbreak occurs. Severely scouring lambs may also need oral electrolyte solution to correct dehydration; give in a separate feed, not with milk.
Identifying whether coccidiosis is the cause

E. coli scour: yellowy white, lambs under 5 days old. Rotavirus: grey-white watery scour, 5-14 days old. Cryptosporidium: pale watery scour, 3-14 days old. Coccidiosis: dark, sometimes bloody scour, 3-8 weeks old. When in doubt, submit a faecal sample to your vet — the treatment is different for each and overcalling coccidiosis will not address the underlying cause if it is something else.
Reducing contamination pressure
Change bedding frequently and remove contaminated straw from pens where lambs have been scouring.
Move lambs onto fresh pasture as early as weather permits. The outdoor dilution effect on oocysts is the single most effective control.
Avoid creep feeders and water troughs that lambs can contaminate with their feet.
Do not introduce unrelated groups into a pen that has had a coccidiosis outbreak without a full clean and dry period.

Herds with recurring problems each spring may benefit from prophylactic diclazuril at housing or at three weeks of age. Discuss this with your vet; prophylaxis is appropriate when the history justifies it, not as a routine.
